worth_it_explanation: Curation through PubMatic Connect packages a publisher's audience and inventory into deals that buyers can find and activate, so it sits between the inventory and the demand that pays for it. A curated package mapped to the wrong inventory, or one left to drift as the audience and the demand around it change, sends a buyer a deal that no longer delivers what it promised, which costs fill and trust rather than showing up as an obvious error. The check is a review of the curated packages a publisher already has in place, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a specific package and the mapping or refresh decision behind it.
intro: Curation is the practice of packaging a publisher's inventory and audience data into deals that buyers can discover and activate, and PubMatic offers it through the Connect platform alongside the deal mechanics of any sell-side platform (SSP). This subcategory is about the curated packages themselves, whether each one points at the inventory and audience it claims to, and whether the packages stay accurate as the inventory and demand around them change. These checks confirm that curated deals are deliberate, correctly mapped, and maintained rather than set once and left to drift.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are not mapped to the inventory and audience they claim
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A curated package that points at the wrong inventory or carries a stale audience segment sends a buyer a deal that does not deliver what its name and description promise. The buyer either underspends because the deal rarely matches, or spends against inventory that does not fit the brief, and either way the deal underperforms while the mismatch stays hidden behind a package that looks correctly configured.

educate.base:
A curated package is a deal a publisher builds by combining a slice of inventory with one or more audience or contextual data segments, then exposing it to a buyer under a deal ID. PubMatic builds these through the Connect platform, and the underlying mechanics are the same private marketplace (PMP) deals that any sell-side platform (SSP) carries on the bidstream. The package is only as good as the two things it joins, so the audit question is whether the inventory targeting and the audience segment behind each package actually describe what the package name and description say they do.

Two kinds of mismatch surface here. The first is inventory: a package named for a section, a format, or a device targets the wrong ad units, includes inventory it should exclude, or excludes inventory it should cover. A buyer activating a 'sports section, in-article video' package expects video on sports pages, so a package whose targeting also sweeps in display on unrelated sections delivers against the wrong impressions. The second is audience: the data segment attached to the package no longer matches its label, points at an empty or near-empty segment, or applies a segment the buyer did not agree to. In Prebid-based header bidding, segment data travels in the standard OpenRTB fields and carries a taxonomy and bidder permissions, so a segment that is mislabeled or improperly permissioned reaches buyers it should not, or fails to reach the buyer it was meant for.

The way to confirm the mapping is to read each curated package against its own description. For inventory, check that the ad units, formats, geographies, and devices the package targets match what the package claims. For audience, check that the data segment attached is the one named, that it carries the size the buyer was told to expect, and that the permissions allow the intended buyer to act on it.

A few signs point to a curated package that is not mapped to what it claims:
  • The inventory targeting on a package sweeps in ad units, formats, or devices outside what the package name and description describe
  • The audience segment attached does not match the package label, or reports a size far below what the buyer was told to expect
  • Segment permissions allow buyers the package was not meant for, or block the buyer it was built for
  • A package buyers can see and activate has no clear owner who can confirm what inventory and audience it targets
You may want to confirm that each live curated package targets the inventory and audience its name and description promise. One approach is to read the targeting and the attached segment for each package against the buyer-facing description and correct any package where the two diverge.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, curated packages often map to sections, content topics, or formats that buyers recognize, for example a 'breaking news' or 'long-form video' package. You may want to confirm that each section or topic package targets only the inventory it names. Editorial inventory shifts as sections are renamed or retired, and a package built against an old section can target the wrong pages.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, curated packages may join product or category inventory with shopper-intent segments, for example a 'high-intent electronics' package. You may want to confirm that the intent segment attached still reflects the audience it names and that the package targets the commercial pages it claims, rather than sweeping in editorial inventory that dilutes the deal the buyer agreed to.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's detailed configuration documentation for building and mapping curated packages in the Connect platform is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each live curated package against its buyer-facing description, checking the inventory it targets and the audience segment it attaches.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic platform UI
steps: Open the curation or deals area of the PubMatic platform and list the active curated packages with their deal IDs and buyer-facing descriptions.
For each package, open its inventory targeting and record the ad units, formats, geographies, and devices it covers.
For each package, record the audience or contextual data segment attached, its reported size, and which buyers are permitted to act on it.
Compare the targeting and segment for each package against the package name and description, and note any package where the two diverge.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic reporting
steps: Build a report by deal or curated package with impressions, fill rate, and average eCPM.
Note any package with very low impressions or fill, which can point to a segment that is empty or targeting that rarely matches, then trace it back to the mapping.
Record which packages deliver against the inventory and audience their description names and which do not.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each curated package, whether its inventory targeting and attached audience segment match its buyer-facing name and description, and whether segment permissions allow the intended buyer. A package whose targeting and segment match its description and whose permissions fit the intended buyer passes. A package whose targeting sweeps in inventory outside its description, whose segment is mislabeled or near-empty, or whose permissions reach the wrong buyers is a fail recorded with the package and the mismatch found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the inventory targeting, the attached segment, and the permissions for each curated package above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs curated packages whose inventory targeting or attached audience segment does not match what the package name and description promise to buyers. A curated package joins a slice of inventory with a data segment under a deal ID, so a mismatch on either side sends a buyer a deal that delivers against the wrong impressions or a segment that no longer fits its label, and the deal underperforms while looking correctly configured. {{voice_recommend}} reading the inventory targeting and the attached segment for each live package against its buyer-facing description, confirming the segment size and the buyer permissions, and correcting any package where the two diverge. After the change, recheck fill and average price on the corrected packages to confirm they deliver what their description claims.

audit_checks.title: Curated packages are not maintained and have gone stale
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Curated packages are built once and then left in place, so the inventory, the audience segments, and the buyer demand around them keep changing while the package stays fixed. A package that is never reviewed accumulates retired inventory, expired or shrinking segments, and dormant deals, which dilutes the value a buyer sees and leaves a publisher carrying packages that no longer earn the demand they were built to attract.

educate.base:
A curated package is not a set-and-forget object. The inventory it targets changes as sections launch and retire and as new ad units and formats come online. The audience segments it attaches change as data partners refresh them, as segment sizes grow or shrink, and as consent and identity signals shift the addressable share. The buyer demand it was built to attract changes as campaigns end and budgets move. A package that was accurate when it was built drifts away from accuracy as all three move underneath it.

Drift shows up in a few ways. A package keeps targeting inventory that has been retired, so its addressable supply shrinks without anyone adjusting it. A segment attached to a package expires or thins out, so the package still carries the label but delivers a fraction of the audience it once did. A curated deal goes dormant, with a buyer who has stopped activating it, yet stays live and visible alongside the packages that still earn demand. None of these throw an error. The package looks the same in the interface while the value behind it erodes.

Maintenance is a periodic review rather than a one-time setup. The review reads each live package for the freshness of its inventory targeting, the current size and expiry of its attached segments, and whether the deal is still active for the buyer it was built for. Packages that have drifted get corrected, refreshed, or retired, and the publisher keeps a record of when each package was last reviewed so the next review knows where to start.

A few signs point to curated packages that have gone stale:
  • Packages target ad units, sections, or formats that have since been retired or renamed
  • Attached audience segments have expired, shrunk well below their original size, or no longer refresh on the schedule the buyer expects
  • Deals sit live and visible with little or no recent activation from the buyer they were built for
  • No record exists of when each curated package was last reviewed, so there is no way to tell which packages are current
You may want to set a periodic review for the curated packages so each one is checked against current inventory, current segment sizes, and current buyer activity. One approach is to review the packages on a regular cadence and retire or refresh any that no longer reflect the inventory, the audience, or the demand they were built for.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, sections and content topics shift often, so a package built against a section that has since been merged or retired keeps a label that no longer matches the inventory behind it. You may want to review the section and topic packages on a regular cadence so each one tracks the current content structure rather than the structure it was built against.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, shopper-intent and product-category segments age quickly because purchase intent is time-bound, so a seasonal or campaign segment attached to a package can thin out once the season or campaign passes. You may want to review the intent-based packages on a cadence that matches how fast those segments turn over, retiring or refreshing the ones tied to a season or campaign that has ended.

learn_more.source_title: First Party Data for Header Bidding

educate.learn_more_note: PubMatic's documentation for reviewing and managing the lifecycle of curated packages in the Connect platform is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review each live curated package for the freshness of its inventory targeting, the current size and expiry of its attached segments, and recent buyer activity.

pubmatic_ui.label: PubMatic platform UI
steps: Open the curation or deals area of the PubMatic platform and list every active curated package with the date it was created or last edited.
For each package, check whether the inventory it targets still exists. Retired or renamed ad units and sections leave a package pointing at supply that is gone.
For each package, record the attached segment's current size and expiry date, and flag any segment that has expired or shrunk well below its original size.
Record whether each package has a documented last-reviewed date, and note packages that have never been reviewed since they were built.

pubmatic_reporting.label: PubMatic reporting
steps: Build a report by deal or curated package with impressions and revenue over a recent window.
Identify packages with little or no recent activity, which can point to a dormant deal or a segment that has thinned out.
Record which packages still earn demand and which have gone quiet, so the review can retire or refresh the dormant ones.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for each curated package, whether its inventory targeting still resolves to live inventory, whether its attached segments are current rather than expired or thinned, whether the deal still draws recent buyer activity, and whether a last-reviewed date exists. Packages that target live inventory, carry current segments, draw recent activity, and have a recent review date pass. Packages targeting retired inventory, carrying expired or thinned segments, sitting dormant, or with no review on record are a fail recorded with the package and the form of drift found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the inventory freshness, the segment size and expiry, the recent activity, and the last-reviewed date for each curated package above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} carries curated packages that have not been reviewed since they were built, so some target retired inventory, carry expired or thinned audience segments, or sit dormant with no recent buyer activity. Curated packages drift as the inventory, the segments, and the demand around them change, and a package left in place keeps its label while the value behind it erodes. {{voice_recommend}} setting a periodic review of the curated packages that checks each one against current inventory, current segment size and expiry, and recent buyer activity, then retiring or refreshing the packages that have drifted and recording a last-reviewed date for each. After the review, the live packages reflect the inventory, audience, and demand they were built to attract.
